narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try based on academic records, test scores, and background details like yours. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 40 (above the average 36.7), upper-middle family income, full-time student status, and lower year 1 and year 3 school decile rankings of 2 and 1 respectively (compared to averages around 5.5-5.7)—and estimated a 40% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. Among all the factors in your profile, three stood out as having the strongest influence on this decision: your LSAT score, your year 3 school decile ranking, and your family income bracket. Your strong LSAT performance was the most powerful positive factor, significantly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar, which worked in your favor despite the overall outcome. In contrast, your year 3 decile ranking—placing you in the bottom tenth of your school—had the next biggest impact, strongly pulling the prediction toward failure by signaling weaker later academic performance relative to peers. Your upper-middle family income provided a moderate boost toward the passing side, likely reflecting access to resources that support success, though it wasn't enough to offset the concerns. Compared to typical students in the dataset, where most are full-time like you (over 92%), your grades in key years lagged behind the middle-of-the-pack averages, amplifying the model's worry about bar exam readiness. While your LSAT shines brighter than most, the dip in school deciles raised red flags that outweighed it here. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps leveraging your LSAT strength in targeted prep for the bar, and reapply after demonstrating improved academic consistency—many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps.
